metadata.performed_by: LLM-assisted extraction from PDF reference sections
source_papers_analysed: Allen et al. (2014) — Understanding workplace meetings
Šlapáková Losová & Orel (2025) — VR as engagement driver
Grabowski et al. (2024) — Group dynamics in the metaverse
Murugavel & Reiter-Palmon (2023) — Integrating workplace meetings and creativity
Wolf et al. (2023) — Making every meeting count
Cionea et al. (2021) — Dialogue orientations in workplace meetings
Köhler et al. (2023) — Culture-driven scripts for meetings
Beck et al. (2025) — Toward a strategic perspective of meeting participation
notes: Minimum inclusion threshold: IC1 must be satisfied PLUS at least one of IC2 or IC3.
Papers already in screened_results.csv are excluded from this list (no duplicates).
preliminary_decision values: 'include' = clearly meets threshold; 'flag' = uncertain, needs full-text check; 'exclude' = clearly fails.
scopus_search.query uses Scopus field codes: DOI(), TITLE(), AUTH(), PUBYEAR, SRCTITLE().
Recommended workflow: (1) run all 'include' DOI queries first; (2) run 'flag' queries and decide on full-text; (3) ignore 'exclude' entries.

candidates.title: The key features of workplace meetings: Conceptualizing the why, how, and what of meetings at work
candidates.justification: Directly addresses the key structural features (why, how, what) of workplace meetings as its primary subject. Highly relevant to facet codebook construction (Step 5.2).

candidates.title: Meeting talk: An introduction
candidates.justification: Foundational overview of workplace meeting talk as a structured communicative form. Cited by three source papers; directly supports dimension extraction.

candidates.title: Meetings matter: Effects of team meetings on team and organizational success
candidates.justification: Examines meeting characteristics as predictors of team/organizational outcomes. Directly relevant to effectiveness dimensions (IC4) and structural analysis (IC3).

candidates.title: Meeting design characteristics and attendee perceptions of staff/team meeting quality
candidates.authors: Cohen, M.; Rogelberg, S.; Allen, J.; Luong, A.
candidates.venue: Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice
candidates.justification: Examines specific meeting design characteristics (IC3) and their link to quality perceptions (IC4). Directly feeds facet codebook.

candidates.title: Perceived meeting effectiveness: The role of design characteristics
candidates.authors: Leach, D.J.; Rogelberg, S.G.; Warr, P.B.; Burnfield, J.L.
candidates.justification: Operationalizes meeting design characteristics empirically. Complements SB004 and directly supports IC3/IC4.

candidates.title: "Not another meeting!" Are meeting time demands related to employee well-being?
candidates.authors: Rogelberg, S.G.; Leach, D.J.; Warr, P.B.; Burnfield, J.L.
candidates.justification: Empirical study on meeting demands and well-being. IC1 and IC4 satisfied. Foundational meeting science reference cited by multiple source papers.

candidates.title: Organizational meetings: Formats and informational requirements
candidates.justification: Directly addresses meeting formats (IC2 — a classification scheme) and informational requirements (IC3). Title contains IC1 keyword 'organizational meetings'. High relevance to faceted taxonomy.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Organizational meetings") AND AUTH(Volkema) AND PUBYEAR = 1995

candidates.title: The meeting as a neglected social form in organizational studies
candidates.justification: Seminal theoretical paper conceptualizing meetings as a distinct organizational form. IC1 and IC3 satisfied. Foundational for any meeting taxonomy.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("meeting as a neglected social form") AND AUTH(Schwartzman)

candidates.title: Meeting analysis: Findings from research and practice
candidates.authors: Romano, N.C., Jr.; Nunamaker, J.F., Jr.
candidates.venue: 34th Annual Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences
candidates.justification: Comprehensive review of meeting research covering formats, purposes, and findings. IC1, IC2, IC3 clearly met. FLAGGED due to EC5 risk: conference proceedings — check if full research paper (accepted at peer-reviewed HICSS) or just an index entry.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Meeting analysis") AND AUTH(Romano) AND PUBYEAR = 2001

candidates.title: Genres of organizational communication: A structurational approach to studying communication and media
candidates.justification: Proposes genre-based classification of organizational communication forms, including meetings. IC1 and IC3 satisfied. Theoretical foundation for meeting type differentiation.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Genres of organizational communication") AND AUTH(Yates) AND PUBYEAR = 1992

candidates.title: The meeting genre across cultures: Insights from three German-American collaborations
candidates.authors: Köhler, T.; Cramton, C.D.; Hinds, P.J.
candidates.justification: Applies the meeting-as-genre framework comparatively across cultures, revealing structural dimensions of meeting norms. IC1, IC2 (genre = classification framework), IC3 all satisfied.

candidates.title: Meeting effectiveness and task performance: meeting size matters
candidates.authors: Allen, J.A.; Tong, J.; Landowski, N.
candidates.justification: Examines group size as a structural meeting dimension (IC3) linked to effectiveness (IC4). Size is a candidate facet for the taxonomy.

candidates.title: Let's get this meeting started: Meeting lateness and actual meeting outcomes
candidates.authors: Allen, J.A.; Lehmann-Willenbrock, N.; Rogelberg, S.G.
candidates.justification: Empirical study on meeting process dimension (lateness) and outcomes. IC1 and IC4 satisfied. High citation count across source papers.

candidates.title: Manager-led group meetings: A context for promoting employee engagement
candidates.justification: Addresses meeting leadership structure (IC3: leader-led vs. other) and meeting engagement as effectiveness dimension (IC4).

candidates.title: The entitativity underlying meetings: Meetings as key in the lifecycle of effective workgroups
candidates.justification: Conceptualizes meetings in relation to group cohesion and effectiveness. IC1 and IC4 satisfied; group configuration (IC3) is a candidate facet.

candidates.title: Business meetings in the metaverse: Stakeholder views evolve
candidates.justification: Addresses business meetings in VR/metaverse context (IC1, IC4). Directly relevant to VR suitability dimension (RQ4).

candidates.title: Perceiving strategic meeting interaction
candidates.justification: Develops a framework for strategic interaction types in meetings (IC3). Relevant to the interaction-type facet of the taxonomy.

candidates.title: Public meeting facilitation: A naïve theory analysis of crisis meeting interaction
candidates.authors: Beck, S.J.; Littlefield, R.S.; Weber, A.J.
candidates.justification: Crisis meeting as a distinct meeting type with specific structural characteristics (IC3) and facilitation requirements (IC4). Adds a meeting type to RQ3 matrix.

candidates.title: Virtual team meetings: An analysis of communication and context
candidates.authors: Anderson, A.H.; McEwan, R.; Bal, J.; Carletta, J.
candidates.justification: Analyzes communication patterns and contextual factors in virtual meetings (IC1, IC3). Directly relevant to the modality/medium facet and VR suitability (RQ4).

candidates.title: Putting the 'group' in group meetings: Entitativity in face-to-face and online meetings
candidates.venue: Research on Managing Groups and Teams
candidates.justification: Examines meeting format differences (face-to-face vs. online) and group cohesion dimensions (IC3). The FTF vs. virtual modality is a key facet candidate.

candidates.title: Virtual work meetings during the COVID-19 pandemic: The good, bad, and ugly
candidates.authors: Karl, K.A.; Peluchette, J.V.; Aghakhani, N.
candidates.justification: Empirical analysis of virtual meeting characteristics and outcomes during COVID-19. IC1, IC3 (meeting format/modality), IC4 all satisfied. Relevant to modality facet.

candidates.title: Committee management: Guidelines from social science research
candidates.justification: Early empirical-theoretical synthesis on committee (meeting) management guidelines and structural factors. IC1 and IC3 satisfied. Foundational reference.

candidates.title: Our love/hate relationship with meetings: Relating good and bad meeting behaviors to meeting outcomes, engagement, and exhaustion
candidates.authors: Lehmann-Willenbrock, N.; Allen, J.A.; Belyeu, D.
candidates.justification: Examines behavioral meeting characteristics (IC3) and their links to engagement and exhaustion (IC4). Behavioral dimension is a candidate facet.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("love hate relationship with meetings") AND AUTH(Lehmann-Willenbrock) AND PUBYEAR = 2016

candidates.title: Linking pre-meeting communication to meeting effectiveness
candidates.authors: Allen, J.A.; Lehmann-Willenbrock, N.; Landowski, N.
candidates.justification: Pre-meeting structure as a temporal meeting dimension (IC3) linked to effectiveness (IC4). Temporal structure is a candidate facet.

candidates.title: Temporal coordination through communication: Using genres in a virtual start-up organization
candidates.authors: Im, H.-G.; Yates, J.; Orlikowski, W.
candidates.justification: Examines communication genres in a virtual organization context (IC3 for genre as classification). IC1 is partial — organizational communication broadly, with meetings as one form. FLAGGED: check whether meetings are a primary focus or incidental context.

candidates.title: The science and fiction of meetings
candidates.authors: Rogelberg, S.G.; Scott, C.; Kello, J.
candidates.justification: Addresses meeting science broadly (IC1, IC4). FLAGGED for EC5: MIT Sloan Management Review is a practitioner-oriented publication without formal peer review. Verify in Scopus whether it is indexed as a peer-reviewed source.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("science and fiction of meetings") AND AUTH(Rogelberg) AND PUBYEAR = 2007

candidates.title: In conference
candidates.justification: Foundational early paper on group interaction in conferences/meetings (IC1, IC3). FLAGGED for EC5: Harvard Business Review is a practitioner publication. Also very old (1954) — likely not indexable in Scopus. Verify.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("In conference") AND AUTH(Bales) AND PUBYEAR = 1954

candidates.title: How to run a meeting
candidates.justification: Classic practitioner guide on meeting facilitation and structure (IC1, IC4). FLAGGED for EC5: HBR is not peer-reviewed. Likely not in Scopus; check for citations to it rather than indexing.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("How to run a meeting") AND AUTH(Jay) AND PUBYEAR = 1976

candidates.title: The science of workplace meetings: Integrating findings, building new theoretical angles, and embracing cross-disciplinary research
candidates.justification: Integrative overview of meeting science (IC1, IC4). FLAGGED for EC5: the volume/page range suggests this may be an editorial introduction to a special issue rather than an original research article. Verify in Scopus — if it is an editorial preface, exclude.

candidates.title: Meetings across cultures: Cultural differences in meeting expectations and processes
candidates.venue: The Cambridge Handbook of Meeting Science
candidates.justification: Examines cross-cultural meeting structural differences (IC1, IC3). FLAGGED for EC5: book chapter rather than peer-reviewed journal article. Likely not indexed in Scopus. Useful as grey literature supplement per RESEARCH_METHOD.md.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Meetings across cultures") AND AUTH(Köhler) AND PUBYEAR = 2015

candidates.title: The Cambridge Handbook of Meeting Science
candidates.authors: Allen, J.A.; Lehmann-Willenbrock, N.; Rogelberg, S.G. (Eds.)
candidates.justification: Entire edited volume — excluded by EC5 (book). Individual chapters may qualify (see SB030 and source papers citing chapters). Do not search as a single item; instead, identify and separately evaluate individual chapters listed in source paper reference sections.

candidates.title: Cognitive organization and identity maintenance in multicultural teams: A discourse analysis of decision-making meetings
candidates.justification: Discourse analysis of decision-making meetings (IC1). Examines cognitive and identity dimensions in meetings (IC3). 'Decision-making' is a key meeting type for RQ3 matrix.

candidates.title: Bales' IPA categories and meeting talk: Interaction process analysis revisited for the study of workplace meetings
candidates.volume_issue: Interaction Process Analysis: A Method for the Study of Small Groups
candidates.justification: General small groups methodology book. IC1 fails: not primarily about professional workplace meetings. EC5: book. Exclude.

candidates.title: Virtual teams: Effects of technological mediation on team performance
candidates.authors: Driskell, J.; Radtke, P.; Salas, E.
candidates.venue: Group Dynamics: Theory Research and Practice
candidates.justification: IC1 fails: focuses on virtual team performance broadly, not workplace meetings as the primary unit of analysis. Meeting format is incidental. Exclude.

candidates.title: Virtual teams research: 10 years, 10 themes, and 10 opportunities
candidates.authors: Gilson, L.L.; Maynard, M.T.; Jones Young, N.C.; Vartiainen, M.; Hakonen, M.
candidates.justification: IC1 fails: review of virtual teams research generally, not meeting-focused. No taxonomy or structural analysis of meetings. Exclude.

recommended_next_steps: 1. Run all 'include' DOI queries in Scopus to confirm indexing and retrieve full citation data.
2. For DOI-confirmed papers, check document type in Scopus (Article / Review / Book Chapter) — exclude if Book Chapter or Editorial.
3. Run 'flag' DOI/title queries; retrieve document type and check if paper is an editorial, book chapter, or practitioner piece (EC5).
4. For any 'include' or 'flag' paper confirmed in Scopus, add it to screened_results.csv with Selection_Decision = 'Included (snowball round 1)'.
5. Once round 1 papers are confirmed, run a new backward snowball on their reference sections for round 2.
6. Stop snowballing when no new papers meeting IC1 + (IC2 or IC3) are found (saturation criterion per Wohlin 2014).
